title: The Feature Creep Saga
description: A simple feature request spirals out of control. Navigate stakeholder demands and protect your sanity.
author: ERB Team

initial-scope.prompt: Product Manager asks: 'Can we add a simple export button to download data as CSV?' Estimated: 2 days. How do you respond?

options.label: Add Buffer
options.description: Say 4 days to account for unknowns

first-creep.prompt: Day 1: PM stops by. 'Oh, can it also export as Excel? Stakeholders prefer that.' What do you say?

options.label: Sure, No Problem
options.description: Excel isn't that different from CSV, right?

options.label: That's Additional Scope
options.description: Flag that this changes the estimate

scope-revealed.prompt: Your questions reveal: they want CSV, Excel, PDF, scheduled exports, and email delivery. The 'simple button' is now a reporting system. What do you do?

options.label: Propose Phases
options.description: Break it into MVP (CSV button) and future phases

buffer-tested.prompt: With your buffer, you finish early. PM asks: 'Since you have extra time, can we also add PDF export?' What do you say?

options.label: Use the Buffer
options.description: You did finish early, might as well add it

options.label: Bank the Time
options.description: Deliver early, save buffer for actual surprises

second-creep.prompt: Day 2: 'Can exports include charts? And maybe schedule them to run nightly?' You're already behind. What now?

options.label: Push Through
options.description: Work overtime to hit the original deadline

options.label: Raise the Flag
options.description: Escalate that scope has tripled but timeline hasn't

ending-boundaries.title: The Boundary Setter
ending-boundaries.summary: By flagging scope changes immediately, you kept the project on track. PM had to prioritize. You delivered CSV on time, with Excel documented as a follow-up. Trust increased because expectations matched reality.

ending-phased.title: The Strategist
ending-phased.summary: Your phased approach let stakeholders see progress quickly with the CSV MVP. They used it immediately and realized PDF wasn't actually needed. You saved weeks of work by shipping small and learning.

ending-honest.title: The Realist
ending-honest.summary: Your 3-week estimate shocked them. They pushed back initially, but you walked through the complexity. In the end, it took 3.5 weeks. You were 2 days off - not 2.5 weeks like the original estimate would have been.

ending-no-buffer.title: The People Pleaser
ending-no-buffer.summary: You used your buffer on extras. Then a real problem emerged - a data format edge case. With no buffer left, you missed the deadline anyway. Next time, PM expects the same 'bonus features' and there's no padding.
lessons: Buffers are for unknowns, not extras
Setting expectations is part of the job

ending-disciplined.title: The Professional
ending-disciplined.summary: You delivered early and banked the time. When a bug appeared in production, you had room to fix it without stress. PM learned that early delivery means quality, not spare capacity for feature creep.

ending-burnout.title: The Martyr
ending-burnout.summary: You crunched for a week. The feature shipped 'on time' but was buggy. You were exhausted and resentful. PM thought it was easy and asked for more next sprint. The cycle continued until you burned out.

ending-late-rescue.title: The Late Learner
ending-late-rescue.summary: Your escalation was awkward - everyone was already expecting the feature. The deadline slipped, trust was damaged. But you learned: scope discussions happen at the START, not the middle.
lessons: Late escalation is better than silent failure
